coredata.dc:title: Red-emitting carbon dots for fluorescent and smartphone-assisted dual-mode detection of Cu(II), Hg(II), and Fe(III) and investigation of the sensing mechanism
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Jun
Fu, Jiayan
Chen, Min
Zhang, Jingsheng
coredata.dc:description: Red-emitting carbon dots (R-CDs) have triggered ever-increasing interest in biochemical sensing due to their deep tissue penetration and minimal biological auto-fluorescence interference. Herein, dual-emitting intrinsic R-CDs are fabricated and well-characterized. R-CDs display ratiometric and colorimetric sensing properties for heavy metal ions (HMIs, Cu2+, Hg2+, and Fe3+), with the corresponding detection limits of 24.5, 74.2, and 170 nM, respectively. Additionally, smartphone imaging colorimetry based on red-green-blue (RGB) value variations is employed to achieve real-time and on-site detection of these HMIs. Crucially, discrimination of these HMIs is facilitated by principal component analysis. Furthermore, R-CDs demonstrate effective optical sensing of these HMIs in tap/lake water and human urine with satisfactory recoveries (95.82–104.8 %). The underlying mechanism of optical sensing, which involves metal ion-triggered aggregation-induced quenching, is elucidated by spectroscopic, morphological analysis, and quantum mechanical calculations. This work presents a feasible real-time approach for the fluorescent and smartphone-assisted dual-mode detection of HMIs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Heavy metal ions
Carbon nanodots
Red emission
Ratiometric detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Discrimination of HMIs by principal component analysis (PCA)
The visual detection of metal ions
Interference studies and discrimination of metal ions by PCA
Mobile apps for HMIs hand-held detection
Real water/lake and human urine samples testing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphological and chemical composition characterization of the R-CDs. (a) TEM image (inset: particle size distribution); (b) the Raman spectrum; (c) XRD patterns; (d) FT-IR spectrum; (e) XPS survey spectrum; and corresponding high-resolution spectra for (f) C 1s, (g) N 1s, (h) O 1s, and (i) S 2p, respectively.

(a) Fluorescence and (b) UV–vis spectra responses to different metal ions. Inset: corresponding fluorescence (irradiation at 365 nm) and visual color changes of R-CDs aqueous solution, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescence responses of R-CDs upon addition of Hg2+ with various concentrations from 0 to 52.13 μM; (b) Stern-Volmer plots of R-CDs for Hg2+, Inset: fluorescence linear plot of R-CDs against Hg2+; (c) Relationship between the I600/I650 and Hg2+ concentration. Inset: the calculation of LOD.
Two-dimensional score plot of R-CDs for the discrimination of three HMIs at different concentrations with (a): 3 μM, (b): 6 μM, (c) 9 μM and (d) 12 μM, respectively.
Portable smartphone-assisted sensing process and the relationship between the (G + B)/R and Hg2+ concentration.

XPS survey spectrum of R-CDs after addition of (a) Hg2+, (b) Cu2+, (c) Fe3+, and corresponding high-resolution spectra of (d) Hg 4f, (e) Cu 2p, (f) Fe 2p, respectively; (g) TEM image of R-CDs in the presence of Hg2+; (h) EDS mapping images of C, N, O, P, S, and Hg elements.
(a) Four model structures (Mn, n = 1–4) containing O, N or/and S elements, (b) the optimized geometries of Mn (n = 1–4) and their aggregates with Hg2+ based on DFT.
Synthesis procedures for R-CDs and visual detection of multiple HMIs.
Analytical results of recovery experiments for Cu2+/Hg2+/Fe3+ detection in tap water (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Red-emitting carbon dots for fluorescent and smartphone-assisted dual-mode detection of Cu(II), Hg(II), and Fe(III) and investigation of the sensing mechanism

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Guizhou Normal University, Guiyang 550001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Guizhou Normal University, Guiyang 550001, P. R. China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: NO. 9 Middle School of Xishui, Xishui 564600, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: NO. 9 Middle School of Xishui, Xishui 564600, P. R. China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Jun Wang and Jiayan Fu contributed equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Red-emitting carbon dots (R-CDs) have triggered ever-increasing interest in biochemical sensing due to their deep tissue penetration and minimal biological auto-fluorescence interference. Herein, dual-emitting intrinsic R-CDs are fabricated and well-characterized. R-CDs display ratiometric and colorimetric sensing properties for heavy metal ions (HMIs, Cu2+, Hg2+, and Fe3+), with the corresponding detection limits of 24.5, 74.2, and 170 nM, respectively. Additionally, smartphone imaging colorimetry based on red-green-blue (RGB) value variations is employed to achieve real-time and on-site detection of these HMIs. Crucially, discrimination of these HMIs is facilitated by principal component analysis. Furthermore, R-CDs demonstrate effective optical sensing of these HMIs in tap/lake water and human urine with satisfactory recoveries (95.82–104.8 %). The underlying mechanism of optical sensing, which involves metal ion-triggered aggregation-induced quenching, is elucidated by spectroscopic, morphological analysis, and quantum mechanical calculations. This work presents a feasible real-time approach for the fluorescent and smartphone-assisted dual-mode detection of HMIs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The continuous development of industry has led to increased environmental problems, with heavy metal ion (HMI) pollution being one of the most severe worldwide [1,2]. In particular, mercuric ion (Hg2+) is a persistent environmental toxin and water pollutant. Due to its non-degradability and bio-incompatibility, Hg2+ can enter the human body via the food chain. Its bioaccumulation factor can be as high as 10 [3], causing severe damage to human health and environmental ecology, even at low concentrations [4]. Cu2+ and Fe3+ are essential elements in organisms, while abnormal levels are closely associated with several diseases, such as cancer and Alzheimer's disease [5,6]. Therefore, it is of great significance to detect HMIs intuitively and feasibly. Sophisticated instrument-based techniques such as inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry and atomic absorption spectrometry, have traditionally been adopted for the qualitative and quantitative determination of HMIs. Besides, electrochemical methods for HMI detection have also been extensively studied [7,8]. However, these off-site methods are time-consuming and expensive, rendering them unsuitable for real-time, on-site detection of HMIs [2].

The fluorescence colorimetric method offers several advantages over traditional instrument-based methods for HMI detection, including low cost, easy operation, visualization, and high sensitivity [2,5,9]. Carbon dots (CDs) are a novel type of biocompatible nanomaterial with exceptional photoluminescence properties, which significantly improves the degree of visualization for on-site and real-time detection of HMI [10,11]. However, the reported CDs were dominated by ultraviolet excitation and emitted blue-green or yellow light [10–13]. This significantly restricts their applications due to interference with the auto-fluorescence of biological tissues [14,15]. On the contrary, red-emitting CDs (R-CDs) with emission wavelengths greater than 600 nm, have minimal autofluorescence, excellent photostability, and deep tissue penetration. They are well-documented to be biocompatibility, making them ideal platforms for practical applications [16,17]. For instance, Zou et al. visually detected Hg2+ in drinking water and milk samples based on blue emission CDs modified with europium (III) complexes [18]. Chen et al. employed R-CDs for Hg2+ sensing [15]. On the other hand, red fluorescence can avoid interference from external lighting. Thus, R-CDs will be suitable for on-site and real-time detection of analytes. So far, the development of intrinsic red emission carbon dots for on-site visual monitoring of HMIs is still urgently needed.

Recently, a smartphone-assisted sensing strategy has been developed and received increasing attention for visual monitoring analytes. This method performs analysis via mobile applications, reads the detection signals via red-green-blue (RGB) color images by a high-resolution integrated camera, and further displays the detection results on the smartphone screen [19,20]. Consequently, the advantages of on-situ detection and portability have led to the utilization of smartphone sensing in the fabrication of standalone detection platforms. The promising colorimetric method has been extensively applied in environmental monitoring, home healthcare, and food safety [21]. For example, Yi et al. employed a smartphone-assisted sensing system based on blue-green CDs-based microarrays to achieve visual and on-site detection of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Pb2+ in river water [22]. Given that the colorimetric method relies on RGB color variations, the wide range of color changes means a higher level of detection accuracy [23]. As a result, the red fluorescent sensing materials could enable the construction of fluorescent and smartphone-assisted dual-mode detection platforms with low background interference and high color recognition [17,24].

Inspired by the numerous advantages of R-CDs and our ongoing investigations into luminescent materials [25–27], we have synthesized intrinsic R-CDs, as shown in Scheme 1 . The R-CDs are modified with O, N, and S surface functional groups, and exhibit dual red emission with excitation-independent behavior, making them ideal for ratiometric and colorimetric sensing of HMIs (Cu2+, Hg2+, Fe3+) using spectroscopic and smartphone imaging colorimetry method. In addition, the three HMIs are well discriminated employing principal component analysis. Furthermore, the R-CDs have been demonstrated to accurately determine HMIs in tap/lake water and human urine solution, with satisfactory recoveries. More importantly, the visual sensing mechanism is proposed in combination with spectroscopic and morphological analysis, as well as energy changes between the HOMO-LUMO band gaps.

Unless otherwise stated, all commercial chemicals were used without further purification. 1,2-diaminobenzene, thiosemicarbazide, phosphoric acid, HEPES (4-(2-hydroxyethyl)-1-piperazineethanesulfonic acid), and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) were obtained from innochem (Beijing, China). The analytical cations source was rooted in the corresponding inorganic salts (HgCl2, NaCl, KCl, MgCl2, CaCl2, CuCl2, CdCl2, FeCl3, CoCl2, NiCl2, BaCl2, ZnCl2, MnSO4, and Pb(NO3)2), respectively. Tap and lake water were obtained from our laboratory and LuoPing Lake in Huaxi District (Guiyang). Human urine was collected from the researcher (Jiayan Fu).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on an FEI Tecnai G2 F20 S-Twin 200 kV (FEI, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained on a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha (Manchester, UK). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were received from an XD-2X/M4600 X-ray diffract meter with Cu Ka radiation (40 kV, 20 mA, λ = 1.5405 Å). Fourier transforms infrared spectra (FT-IR) were carried on a Thermo Scientific Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer (Sugar Land, TX, USA). The Raman spectrum was performed on a LabRAM HR Evolution (Horiba, Japan). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis, UV-2550, Shimadzu, Japan) and FL970 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Techcomp, China) were used to scan UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. Quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime were detected by Edinburgh Instruments FLS1000 near-infrared spectrometer. The solution pH was regulated by HEPES (pH = 6.80, 10 mM) utilizing a PHS-3C pH meter.

R-CDs were prepared according to our previously reported one-pot hydrothermal method [25]. 1,2-diaminobenzene and thiosemicarbazide were employed as carbon, nitrogen, and sulfur sources, respectively, and H3PO4 (1 mL) served as the catalyst. The optimization conditions can be found in the Supporting Information (Fig. S1,2 and Table S1). Briefly, a mixture of 1,2-diaminobenzene (108.20 mg), thiosemicarbazide (91.10 mg), and 10 mL deionized water was ultrasonicated for 30 min to ensure complete dissolution. The mixture was then transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, it was neutralized with NaOH solution to achieve a pH of 7. The resulting purple precipitate was filtered through a 0.22 μM membrane and washed with distilled water. The residues were further purified using a dialysis bag (1000 Da, molecular weight cut-off) for 24 h in C2H5OH. Finally, the purple solid R-CDs were obtained by lyophilization.

The stock solutions of R-CDs (33.3 mg/L) were prepared in ethanol. Metal ions solution (2 mM) and HEPES buffer (pH = 6.80, 10 mM) were prepared in deionized water. R-CDs stock solution (100 μL) was then diluted with HEPES buffer and ethanol to achieve the 2 mL final R-CDs (1.66 mg/L) for spectroscopic measurements and real water/lake and human urine samples testing in HEPES-C2H5OH binary solution (V/V = 1/1). In smartphone app-based experiments, the R-CDs concentration was 6.2 mg/L.

The spectral experiments utilized 2 mL R-CDs (1.66 mg/L) binary solution with or without HMIs (250 μM). The titration curves were plotted by monitoring the changes in emission intensity ratio at 600 nm and 650 nm (RI600/650), representing the relationship between RI600/650 and the concentration of metal ions. The limit of detection (LOD) was determined using the 3σ/S method [28]. The fluorescence quenching coefficient (KSV) was obtained from the Stern-Volmer equation [29] (See supporting information for the calculations of LOD and KSV in detail). The interference studies were conducted by adding Cu2+, Hg2+, or Fe3+ (250 μM) to the R-CDs with other competitive metal ions (250 μM). Fluorescent spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 540 nm by 5 nm spit. All spectral experiments were implemented three times at room temperature.

To estimate the accuracy of the proposed method, utilizing the customized R-CDs (1.66 mg/L) in tap/lake water and human urine solutions with varying concentrations of HMIs (Hg2+, Cu2+, and Fe3+), recovery studies were conducted. The recovery percentage was calculated using the following equation:
Recovery
( 100 % )
=
C exp
C
a d d
× 100
Wherein, Cexp and Cadd represent the different concentrations of HMIs. Cexp is the average concentration in the fortified samples, while Cadd is the added concentration of HMIs in the non-fortified sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discrimination of HMIs by principal component analysis (PCA)
ce:section.ce:para: The study employed the absorbance ratio A285/A540, fluorescence intensity I600, and ratio I600/650 as discriminant components to perform PCA for distinguishing Hg2+, Cu2+, and Fe3+. The discriminant components were obtained from five parallel experiments with different concentrations (3, 6, 9, and 12 μM) of the metals. The data was processed using OriginLab software (2021). The primary separation direction in PCA was created by combining the first and second principal components (PC1 and PC2), with PC1/PC2 serving as the horizontal/vertical coordinates, respectively. A two-dimensional score plot was subsequently produced.

PVA (0.8 g) and R-CDs (20 mg) were dissolved in 20 mL water and 6 mL ethanol, and stirred for 6 h at 80 °C. The mixture was then poured into a culture dish with a diameter of 5 cm. After drying in an oven at 45 °C for 6 h, the resulting R-CDs@PVA composite film was obtained.

The structure, size, and morphology of the R-CDs are investigated using TEM, XRD, and Raman spectra. The TEM image (Fig. 1 a) displays spherical dots that are well separated with an average size of 2.75 nm. The HRTEM image of R-CDs (Fig. S3) presented an unambiguous lattice structure with a crystal spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon [12,16]. The Raman spectrum (Fig. 1b) shows a D/G band at 1375/1442 cm−1, which belongs to the disordered surface and sp2 hybrid framework of R-CDs. The intensity ratio (ID/IG) is found to be 1.40, indicating defective graphitic carbon [30]. Additionally, the XRD pattern exhibits a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 26.30° (Fig. 1c), corresponding to the (002) plane for typical amorphous carbon. This peak results from deformations induced by the heteroatomic dopants in the graphitic structures [16,31]. These existing results jointly demonstrate that the as-prepared R-CDs form an amorphous carbon phase [32].

The functional groups on the surface of R-CDs are inspected in detail. The FT-IR spectrum in Fig. 1d demonstrates the presence of absorption bands at 3400 cm−1 and 1218 cm−1, which are ascribed to stretching vibrations of the –OH and –NH2 groups. The characteristic absorption band of CO/CN/CC is observed at 1506-1648 cm−1 [31,33]. Additionally, the stretching vibrations of the CS, C–N, and C–S bonds are presented at 1445, 1152, and 593 cm−1 [32,34]. The XPS spectra are used to confirm the elemental composition and surface chemical states of the customized R-CDs. The full scan XPS spectrum (Fig. 1e) shows that the R-CDs are composed of carbon (67.78 %), oxygen (20.36 %), nitrogen (4.72 %), sulfur (5.80 %), and phosphorus (1.32 %). The high-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1f) exhibits three fitted peaks at 284.42, 285.94, and 287.82 eV, corresponding to C–C/CC, C–O/C–N/C–S, and CO/CN/CS bonds [31,34], respectively. The N 1s spectrum shows the characteristic pyridinic N (398.45 eV) and pyrrolic N (399.68 eV) [31], as depicted in Fig. 1g. Besides, the O 1s peaks are located in 531.46 and 533.52 eV (Fig. 1h), belonging to C–O and CO bonding [14,35]. Furthermore, the S 2p spectrum can be divided into two peaks at 163.82 and 164.45 eV (Fig. 1i), suggesting the presence of C–Sn–C (n = 1 or 2) and CS bonds [33]. Combining the FT-IR, these observations demonstrate that the surface of the R-CDs contains O, N, and S functional groups, guaranteeing the potential for good sensing performance for HMIs.

The optical properties of the R-CDs are analyzed using UV–vis absorption and fluorescent spectra. The R-CDs exhibit three absorption peaks of CO/CN/CS bonding. The high-energy peak at 285 nm corresponds to the π-π∗ transition, while the low-energy peaks around 540 and 577 nm are attributed to the n-π∗ transition (Fig. S4). Parallelly, two excitation bands are observed at 540 and 570 nm. The R-CDs in an ethanol solution display two emission peaks at 600 nm and 650 nm when excited at 540 nm (Fig. S5, S6), consistent with the FT-IR and XPS analysis. As expected, the R-CDs produce a pink solution when exposed to sunlight, and red luminescence when irradiated with 365 nm, as evidenced by their photographs (Fig. S6, inset) and the corresponding chromaticity coordinate (
x
,
y
) of (0.63, 0.37) (Fig. S7). The R-CDs’ lifetime and absolute fluorescent quantum yield in absolute ethanol are determined to be 2.23 ns (Fig. S8) and 9.87 %, respectively.

Subsequently, the stability of the customized R-CDs is evaluated under a range of conditions. The R-CDs aqueous solution has excellent dispersibility and optical performance. Furthermore, the red emission intensity remains unchanged following constant 365 nm UV irradiation for 60 min with an interval of 5 min (Fig. S9), demonstrating the excellent photostability of the R-CDs. Additionally, the dual-emissive band retains its fluorescent intensity upon the addition of NaCl concentrations ranging from 0 to 2.0 M (Fig. S10), indicating that the R-CDs are highly resistant to high salt environments. Besides, the pH-dependent photoluminescence of R-CDs is investigated. Fig. S11 demonstrates that the emission intensity remains relatively stable across a wide pH range of 5.0–11.0, with a pronounced decrease in a strongly alkaline (pH > 11.0) or lower pH solution (pH < 4.0). In addition, the R-CDs show a stable luminescence behavior in the presence of H2O2 (Fig. S12). These resulting observations demonstrate that the R-CDs have favorable properties, including good photostability, acceptable resistance to oxidation, and insensitivity to changes in pH and salinity. Consequently, they are suitable for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The visual detection of metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: Spectroscopic methods are used to study the responses of R-CDs toward various metal ions. As illustrated in Fig. 2 a, the double red emissions at 600 and 650 nm are almost completely quenched, and the fluorescent color is cancelled upon the addition of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Fe3+. Negligible emission color and spectral responses are observed for other cations. Additionally, the UV–vis spectral changes of R-CDs are examined in the presence of cations (Hg2+, Cu2+, and Fe3+). As demonstrated in Fig. 2b, the low-energy absorptions have disappeared completely. Meanwhile, the solution changes color from pink to yellow, or achromatic (Fig. 2b Inset), demonstrating that the well-designed R-CDs can achieve visual detection of multiple HMIs.

Subsequently, the detailed sensing properties are investigated through fluorescence titration experiments. The red fluorescence intensity gradually decreases with increasing Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0–52.13 μM, as shown in Fig. 3 a. The quenching coefficient (KSV) is calculated as 1.87 × 105 M−1 (Fig. 3b). Furthermore, there is a good linear relationship between the fluorescence quenching degree [(F0 − F)/F0] at 600 nm and Hg2+ concentration. The limit of detection (LOD) is validated as 74.2 nM using the 3σ/S method (Fig. 3c). Similarly, the Cu2+ and Fe3+-induced fluorescence quenching of red emission are also observed (Fig. S13 and 14). Correspondingly, the KSV and LODs of Cu2+/Fe3+ are determined to be 7.6 × 105/2.08 × 105 M−1 (Fig. S15 and 16), and 24.51 (R2 = 0.99)/170 nM (R2 = 0.97) (Fig. S17 and 18), respectively. These results explicitly demonstrate the high sensitivity of R-CDs compared to similar metal ion sensors reported previously, (Table S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference studies and discrimination of metal ions by PCA
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the significant changes in fluorescence intensity due to pH-dependent protonation/deprotonation of detection sites on R-CDs surface, the pH influence on sensing performance for HMIs is evaluated. The results indicate that Cu2+/Hg2+/Fe3+-triggered fluorescence quenching remains constant within the pH range of 6–10 (Fig. S19). Besides, the red emission of R-CDs solutions does not diminish in the presence of Cu2+/Hg2+/Fe3+ and other metal ions, certifying that these competitive ions can not affect their visual detection (Fig. S20). These anti-interference studies confirm the effectiveness of R-CDs in realistic environments.

To discriminate the different responses of R-CDs triggered by Cu2+/Hg2+/Fe3+ within a specific concentration range, the PCA method is employed. The UV–vis profile of R-CDs displays two main absorption peaks at 285 and 540 nm. When metal ions (10/30 μM) are added, A285 increases while A540 decreases or remains unchanged, as presented in Fig. S21. However, the degree of fluorescence quenching at 600 and 650 nm varies dramatically with different concentrations of Cu2+/Hg2+/Fe3+ (10/30 μM) (Fig. S22). Therefore, we adopt three discriminant factors to establish a multidimensional data matrix: the absorbance ratio A285/A540, fluorescence intensity ratio I600/I650, and fluorescence intensity I600. The matrix consists of 3 sensing units, 3 metal ions, and 5 replicates per measurement. As plotted in Fig. 4 a, the plots are well-clustered with a smaller gap among the five samples, when the metal ion concentration was 3 μM. Additionally, a significant difference among the three HMI groups is observed. The PCA score plots (Fig. 4) discriminate between the three ions at concentrations of 6, 9, and 12 μM. The results demonstrate that the R-CDs can accurately differentiate between the three HMIs with varying concentrations [23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mobile apps for HMIs hand-held detection
ce:section.ce:para:
To achieve on-site and real-time detection of HMIs while also considering cost-effectiveness, it may be more feasible to employ smartphone app-based visual analytic techniques instead of ponderous spectrometers [36]. Therefore, we utilize a smartphone app (Color Card Generator) to extract the quantitative information from a photochromic R-CDs HEPES-C2H5OH binary solution. The convenient sensing procedure is displayed in Fig. 5 and 6
. R-CDs are dispersed in a binary solution with and without HMIs. After a 30-min incubation period, smartphone images of the samples are taken under 365 nm excitation. The values (0–255) for the R (red), G (green), and B (blue) pixels are extracted (Table S3). The resulting concentration-responsive coordinates are plotted to represent the changes in (G + B)/R as a function of the metal ion concentrations. Finally, a linear relationship is confirmed through linear fitting. The LODs for Cu2+, Hg2+, and Fe3+ are also determined to be 0.55, 1.66, and 18.7 μM (Fig. S23, 5, S24), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real water/lake and human urine samples testing
ce:section.ce:para: Recovery experiments are conducted to evaluate the practicality and reliability of the visual sensing method in real water samples, including tap water, lake water, and human urine. For instance, in the case of Hg2+ in tap water, recovery experiments are carried out by adding different concentrations (15, 20, and 25 μM) of Hg2+ into the tap water. Table 1 shows the satisfactory recoveries for detecting Hg2+, ranging from 96.82 % to 104.8 %, with relative standard deviations (RSD) of less than 2.5 %. The spiked concentrations of Cu2+ and Fe3+ differ from those of Hg2+ due to their different sensitivities (LODs of 24.5/170 nM). The recoveries and RSD are determined to be 96.30–103.25 % and less than 2 %, respectively. Similarly, the recoveries of Cu2+, Hg2+, and Fe3+ ions in lake water solutions range from 95.82 % to 103.05 %, with an RSD not exceeding 2.8 %. In human urine, the recoveries for Cu2+ and Hg2+ cases, with an RSD of less than 5.2 %, are considered acceptable (Table S4). However, due to the lowest sensitivity for Fe3+, satisfactory recoveries are difficult to obtain. These outcomes indicate that the R-CDs could be employed for early diagnosis of multiple HMI contaminations in real water.

To gain a deeper insight into the visual detection of the three HMIs, the possible sensing mechanism is investigated. It should be noted that the R-CDs powder exhibits any non-fluorescence but shows strong red emission in an ethanol solution (Fig. S5). Furthermore, Fig. S25 demonstrates that the R-CDs solution features excitation-independent behavior due to the molecular state of the R-CDs [37]. However, the emission intensity gradually decreases and is almost completely quenched with the addition of different amounts of water, ranging from 0 % to 90 % (Fig. S26). This can be responsible for the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect resulting from the π-π stacking [38]. The ACQ effect can be further confirmed by photoluminescence of R-CDs doped PVA film (R-CDs@PVA). As illustrated in Fig. S27, the film displays pale or pure red emission under 254/365 nm illumination, which can be attributed to the dispersed state of R-CDs in polymer matrices [39].

The FT-IR spectra of R-CDs containing metal ions Hg2+, Cu2+, and Fe3+ are shown in Fig. S28, 29, and 30. It can be observed that in the presence of metal ions Hg2+, Cu2+, and Fe3+, the stretching vibrations of –NH2/–OH (3400 cm−1) and CN (1594 cm−1) disappear or are severely passivated, indicating interactions between the metal ions and the amino, pyridine/pyrrole nitrogen. Furthermore, the intensity of the CS (1445 cm−1) stretching vibration exhibits a notable shift, suggesting the formation of a coordination complex between the S and the metal ions. To gain further insight into the detecting mechanism, XPS analysis was conducted to analyze the changes in binding energies of N 1s, O 1s, and S 2p in the presence of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Fe3+. As presented in Fig. S31, the N 1s binding energy peak can be divided into two distinct peaks, centered at 399.68 eV and 398.45 eV, corresponding to pyrrolic N and pyridinic N. Following the introduction of these HMIs, a change in the proportion of nitrogen and corresponding binding energy positions is perceived, which is attributed to the electron transfer from N to HMIs [40,41]. As illustrated in the high-resolution spectra of O 1s and S 2p (Fig. S32 and 33), the observed changes are similar to those observed in N 1s spectra in the presence of HMIs. Additionally, the binding energy peaks of Hg 4f, Cu 2p, and Fe 2p (Fig. 6a–c) are observed in XPS full-scan R-CDs spectra after soaking in HMIs solution. Their high-resolution spectra are shown in Fig. 6d–f. The binding energy peaks of Hg2+ 4f7/2 and Hg2+ 4f5/2 are presented at about 100.64 and 104.68 eV [33]. The Cu2+ 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 peaks are located at about 932.73/935.82/943.44 eV and 952.52/955.66/963.05 eV [42], respectively. The two peaks at 712.65 and 725.78 eV correspond to the Fe3+ 2p3/2 [43]. The existing results confirm that the surface functional groups of R-CDs have been coordinated with these HMIs. Furthermore, the TEM images (Fig. 6g) show that the R-CDs form aggregates in the presence of Hg2+. Energy-dispersive X-ray mapping images confirm the presence of Hg in the aggregates (Fig. 6h and Fig. S34). Therefore, combining the IR analysis, deconvolution of XPS spectra, and TEM images, it can be speculated that the coordination interaction leads to the aggregation of R-CDs, resulting in the quenching of red emission.

Based on the density functional theory (DFT), the quantum mechanical calculations are performed by Gaussian 03 packages [44]. To address the uncertainty surrounding the exact structure of R-CDs [37], four conjugated structures are designed as models (abbreviated as Mn, n = 1–4) containing seven rings and O, N, or/and S elements. Their geometries are optimized using the B3LYP/6–311(d,p) basis set and are shown in Fig. 7 . The model molecules are found to adopt a planar configuration, as indicated by the representative dihedral angles in Table S5. The electron distribution for the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMO) of Mn (n = 1–4) is spread across the entire molecular plane. This results in effective electron transfer from HOMO to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMO), giving rise to a strong fluorescence emission. Electronegative atoms (O, N/S) with higher electron density provide suitable coordination sites for HMIs in both the HOMO and LUMO. After coordinating with electron-deficient Hg2+, four model units of R-CDs are perfectly bonded with Hg2+ via the formation of four coordination bonds, leading to the aggregation formation of R-CDs. The electronic distribution state of Mn–Hg is the same as that of Mn HOMO. However, the reduced energy gap (△Egap) makes n-π∗ transitions from O, N/S of R-CDs to Hg2+ more favorable, resulting in their LUMOs being mainly decentralized around the Hg2+ central. Thus, the fluorescence quenching is attributed to the aggregated state of R-CDs and the enhanced electron transfer from model units to Hg2+ [37,45], as shown in Fig.S35.

Those results demonstrate that the visual detection of metal ions is initiated by the coordination interaction, leading to the following two effects: (1) enhanced electron transfer from O, N/S atoms on the R-CDs surface to the metal ions and the nonradioactive recombination [36], and (2) acceleration of the formation of R-CDs aggregates, resulting in fluorescence turning off.

In conclusion, we have synthesized excitation-independent red-emissive R-CDs using the hydrothermal method. Our investigation has revealed that R-CDs feature several key advantages, including dual red-emission, excellent photostability, salt resistance, moderate oxidation resistance, and pH insensitivity. Leveraging their outstanding optical properties, R-CDs can effectively monitor multiple heavy metal ions (Cu2+, Hg2+, Fe3+) in a ratiometric and colorimetric manner, enhancing visualization through spectroscopic and smartphone imaging colorimetry techniques. Notably, the proposed visual detection approach utilizing R-CDs is successful in fluorescent sensing of heavy metal ions in tap/lake water and human urine solutions, with satisfactory recoveries. This research presents a new perspective on developing intrinsic red dual-emissive carbon dots for multimodal and reliable detection of multiple heavy metal ions in environmental settings.
Supplementary information available: the quantum mechanical calculations, the calculation method of KSV and LOD, and other general experimental details.

Jun Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Jiayan Fu: Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Min Chen: Visualization, Investigation, Data curation. Jingsheng Zhang: Software, Investigation.

Jun Wang reports financial support was provided by The Science and Technology Program of Guizhou Province. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant No 22161012); Academic New Seedling Foundation Project of Guizhou Normal University (Grant No. Qianshixinmiao-[2022]07).

ce:section.ce:para:

Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtchem.2024.102331.
